label: InTouch can become a commercially viable managed referral network for complementary service providers.

children.label: The initial startup wedge can support a working managed referral-circle model.
Newly founded or recently funded startups are a viable customer-profile wedge for multi-provider referral circles.
Newly founded or recently funded startups have clustered needs for multiple external services within a short time window.
Multiple complementary service-provider types repeatedly sell into the same startup buying moment.
(P0) These providers encounter situations where their customers need another circle member’s service.
The provider types are non-competitive enough to collaborate without threatening each other’s business.
The expected referral value in the startup wedge is high enough for providers to care about improving referral flow.
Providers in the startup wedge lack an efficient, reliable source of qualified referral leads from complementary service providers.
(P0) Many providers in the startup wedge do not already have enough complementary providers who can refer them relevant startup customers.
(P0) Existing referral-networking options for startup-focused providers require too much work relative to the lead value they produce.
(P0) Complementary providers are a credible source of qualified startup leads because they encounter relevant customer needs before or during their own sales or service work.
(P0) At least one InTouch value mechanism creates enough value in the startup wedge.
(P0) Curated circle formation creates enough value by helping startup-focused providers join a better referral group than they could form themselves.
(P0) Managed referral circles create enough value by generating qualified startup referral leads that would not have happened otherwise.
Referral opportunity surfacing creates enough value by helping startup-focused providers notice valuable intro moments they would otherwise miss.
Referral coordination and accountability creates enough value by increasing follow-through after a startup referral opportunity is identified.
At least one valuable InTouch mechanism can be delivered well enough in the startup wedge.
InTouch can identify promising startup-focused circle compositions with enough accuracy.
InTouch can help startup-focused circles generate qualified referral opportunities that members would not reliably create on their own.
InTouch can surface relevant startup referral opportunities with enough accuracy and context.
InTouch can support startup referral coordination without requiring too much manual effort from members.
Startup-focused providers and circles repeatedly perform the behaviors required for the network to work.
Providers complete the initial circle setup steps needed to make matching and referrals useful.
Providers share enough customer and relationship context for referral opportunities to be identified.
(P0) Providers act on credible referral opportunities when surfaced.
Providers report enough referral outcomes to maintain accountability and learning.
(P0) Referral circles remain active across repeated startup customer interactions, not just onboarding.
There is a repeatable way to form and grow viable referral circles in the startup wedge.
Repeatable channels can reach providers who sell to newly founded or recently funded startups.
The startup-wedge value proposition generates qualified interest from multiple complementary provider types.
(P0) Interested startup-focused providers convert into actual circle participation.
Startup-focused circle formation can be repeated without relying on founder-only personal networks.
Existing startup-focused members create a credible path to additional qualified members or adjacent circles.
The startup wedge can capture enough value after acquisition and operating costs.
Startup-focused providers will pay enough for circle formation, referral value, or workflow improvement.
The cost of identifying, recruiting, and assembling startup-focused circles can support attractive acquisition economics.
The managed operating model can deliver startup-wedge referral value without requiring too much manual work per circle.
Pricing does not damage trust, referral behavior, or perceived neutrality in startup-focused circles.
The initial startup wedge creates a credible path to a meaningful business beyond first proof.
The startup wedge is large and economically attractive enough to support a meaningful business on its own.
The startup wedge can expand across similar startup segments, provider clusters, or geographies.
The startup wedge creates a credible path into adjacent customer-profile wedges.
The managed referral-circle model proven in the startup wedge can be replicated in other customer-profile wedges.
Other customer profiles also create clustered needs for multiple complementary service providers.
Providers serving other customer profiles also lack efficient, reliable sources of qualified referral leads from complementary providers.
The selected InTouch value mechanism can create referral-network value outside the startup wedge.
The method for identifying and assembling promising circles can work beyond startup-focused providers.
InTouch can operate with acceptable privacy, trust, and data-sharing friction across wedges.
Providers can share enough customer and relationship context for InTouch to work without unacceptable privacy concerns.
Customers are not exposed to referral behavior that damages provider trust or customer relationships.
Circle members trust how InTouch uses shared data and maintains neutrality between members.
